info.name: Sanad — Offline-First Field Operations
info.description:
The sync protocol, the conflict scenario, dispatch, and reports.

## Run this first

1. **Demo → Create session** — seeds a throwaway org and sets every variable you need.
2. **The Conflict Scenario** folder — run the three requests in order. That is the whole system in 30 seconds.

## The one thing to understand

A field device never calls a normal REST endpoint to change state. It appends a **mutation** to a local queue and pushes a batch to `POST /sync/push` when it has signal.

Every mutation carries:
- `mutation_id` — a **client-generated** UUID. The idempotency key. Generated on the device precisely so a retry is provably the same act.
- `seq` — a per-device monotonic counter. The order the human *acted*, which is not the order the packets arrived.
- `client_ts` — when it **happened**, in the field. Not when it arrived.
- `base_version` — the row version the device last saw. **This one integer is the entire conflict-detection mechanism.**

item.name: 0 · Demo
Create session (RUN THIS FIRST)

exec: // JOB-1001 is staged at picked_up — one tap from delivered.
// A demo that takes five minutes to reach the interesting part
// is a demo nobody sees the interesting part of.
console.log('Now run the Conflict Scenario folder, in order.');

request.description:
Seeds a throwaway org: 2 drivers, 1 dispatcher, 1 registered device, 5 jobs, van stock. Self-destructs in an hour.

Isolation comes free: a demo session is **just another org**, using the same tenant scoping that separates two real paying customers. There is no special sandbox code path — which is exactly where a vulnerability would hide.

item.name: Run the whole conflict scenario server-side

request.description:
For when you have 30 seconds and will not click through six requests.

Runs the entire Abu Ghraib scenario end-to-end and returns a **narrated, timestamped transcript** — including the resolver's actual reason string, straight from the production code. Nothing here is scripted or simulated.

item.name: Peek behind the curtain

request.description:
The mutation ledger, the outbox rows (watch one flip `pending` → `published`), the conflict decisions with reasons, and the current job versions.

Every row is a real row in Postgres, written by the real sync engine in a real transaction.

item.name: 1 · The Conflict Scenario
item.description:
**Run these three in order.** This is the system.

| time | who | what |
|---|---|---|
| 14:02 | driver | delivers the parcel. **Offline.** |
| 14:30 | dispatcher | sees no update, **reassigns the job** |
| 17:00 | driver | reaches wifi. The delivery syncs, 3 hours late |

**Who wins?**

item.name: Step 1 · Dispatcher REASSIGNS the job (14:30)

exec: pm.test('reassigned — the row version has moved', () => {
console.log('Version moved to ' + job.version + '. The device is now stale and does not know it.');

request.description:
The office assumes the driver is stuck and hands the job to someone else.

This is a **reasonable** decision given what the office can see. It is about to turn out to be wrong.

Note: the dispatcher goes through the **same optimistic-version check a device does**. The office is not privileged in the concurrency model — it is just another writer, and it can lose a race too. Treating the office as automatically authoritative is precisely the assumption that produces a delivered parcel marked as still out for delivery.

item.name: Step 2 · Driver's DELIVERY syncs, 3 hours late (17:00)

exec: const r = Math.random()*16|0, v = c === 'x' ? r : (r&0x3|0x8);
pm.test('FIELD_WINS — the delivery beat the reassignment', () => {

request.description:
**The moment of truth.**

Note `base_version` — the version the device saw before it went dark. The office has moved the row since. `base_version !== server_version`, so a **conflict is detected**, and that one integer comparison is the entire mechanism.

Note `client_ts` — **14:02**, when the parcel physically changed hands. Not now.

The resolver runs:

> `delivered` is **PHYSICAL** (rank 3). The server holds a **PROGRESS** state after an office reassignment (rank 1). **Physical truth outranks administrative intent.**

**The delivery wins.** Read the `reason` field in the response — it is the actual output of the production resolver, and it is what the dispatcher will be shown.

item.name: Step 3 · The full history — every hand that touched this job

request.description:
Every mutation, from the field and the office, with **`dark_seconds`** on each — the gap between when a person **acted** and when we **heard**.

That number exists only because every mutation stores *both* `client_ts` and `received_at`. **A normal API throws the first one away**, because a normal API assumes the two are the same moment.

This is the endpoint that turns *"why does this row look like this?"* from a shrug into an answer.

item.name: 2 · Sync protocol
Push — a batch from a device that was dark all afternoon

request.description:
**Partial success is the NORMAL outcome, not an error.** A batch of 40 from a driver offline all afternoon might land 37 applied, 2 duplicates from a retry, and 1 conflict where the office cancelled a job. That is a **200** — the sync worked. The *mutations* are what have verdicts.

Mutations are applied in **`seq` order**, not arrival order. Sent shuffled (a retry, a partial upload), `deliver` before `pickup` would be rejected as an illegal transition and **a real delivery would be lost to a networking artefact**.

item.name: Push the SAME batch again (idempotency)

request.description:
**Same `mutation_id`. Run it as many times as you like.**

Returns `duplicate`. The cash is counted **once**.

This is not theoretical. On a bad link the device uploads, the server applies it, and the **response is lost on the way back**. The device concludes it failed and retries. Without dedupe, every dropped ACK becomes a double delivery, a double stock decrement, and a double cash entry.

`mutation_id` is the **PRIMARY KEY** of the mutation ledger and **UNIQUE** on the cash ledger. Not a `SELECT` — a `SELECT`-then-`INSERT` races between two API replicas. **Only the unique index is atomic across processes.**

item.name: Pull — what changed while I was dark

request.description:
Scoped to the driver's **own** assignments — derived from his token, never from a parameter. There is no query string he could send that would widen it.

That is the BOLA defence, closed at the **query** rather than in a middleware someone can forget to attach.

item.name: 3 · Security probes (these SHOULD fail)
item.description: Every one of these is an attack. Every one is refused. Run them.

item.name: BOLA — mutate a job assigned to another driver

request.description:
**Expected: `rejected` — "This item is not assigned to you."**

The job exists. The caller is authenticated. **Neither of those means he is allowed to touch THIS ONE.**

And the attempt is **recorded in the ledger**. A rejected BOLA attempt is a security event, and throwing it away means you never find out you are being probed.

item.name: Mass assignment — set my own price

request.description:
**Expected: refused as a mass-assignment attempt.**

Note the attack: the malicious fields are wrapped inside a **genuine delivery**. The delivery is physical, so it would win under the core rule — and the `price` change would ride along on a legitimate decision.

It does not, because **the allowlist check runs FIRST, before any resolution logic.**

**Order of checks is a security property.**

item.name: Wrong token type — device token on a dashboard endpoint

request.description:
**Expected: 401 `WRONG_TOKEN_TYPE`.**

A device token lives for **90 days** — it has to, because a driver is dark for nine hours and cannot refresh. That is only safe because it authorises so little: it opens `/sync` and **nothing else**.

Without this check, a 90-day token would read the entire fleet, and the whole scope/lifetime argument collapses.

> **The scope of a credential should be inversely proportional to its lifetime.**

item.name: Tenant escape — supply my own org_id

request.description:
**Expected: 400 — refused, and logged.**

`org_id` **always** comes from the verified token. There is a middleware that rejects any request that even *tries* to supply one — **loudly**, because a client sending `org_id` is either a bug worth finding or an attack worth logging.

item.name: 4 · Dispatch & reports
The board
Conflict inbox — what the system refused to guess at

request.description:
Escalations. Each has the device's version and the server's version attached, and the system **deliberately did not act**.

An escalation is not a failure of the design — **it is the design refusing to invent an answer it does not have.**

> A **rising** escalation rate means reality contains a case the rules do not cover. That is the system telling you the truth.
>
> The failure mode to fear is an escalation rate of **zero**, achieved by guessing.

item.name: Fleet health — who is dark, and for how long

request.description:
A device that is *dark* is not necessarily broken — that is the **normal condition** in the field. What matters is whether it comes back.

A device in `long_dark` is carrying unsynced work **that nobody can see yet**. The system cannot tell whether that driver is in a coverage hole or has gone home with twelve deliveries on his phone — but it can **tell you that it cannot tell**, which is infinitely better than a dashboard that quietly shows everything as fine.

item.name: Dark-time report — the number nobody else has

request.description:
**How long, typically, between a driver DOING something and the system HEARING about it?** p50, p95, worst case, per driver.

This exists **only** because every mutation stores both `client_ts` and `received_at`. A normal API throws the first one away.

And it is genuinely useful to the business, not just a nice graph: a driver whose **p95 dark time is six hours** is working a route with **no coverage**. That is a fact about the physical world, derived from database timestamps — and you can act on it.

item.name: Cash reconciliation

request.description:
`outstanding = collected − remitted`. The report where money actually goes missing.

Correct **by construction**: `cash_entries` is append-only and `mutation_id` is `UNIQUE`. A driver who syncs the same batch twice on a bad network **physically cannot** double-count a collection — the database refuses the second insert.
